hints.text: Very **mountainous, fjords** along most of the coast
Almost all houses **wooden**: red (like SE/FI), **black more common than elsewhere**, white frequent; wooden houses even in suburbs (SE/FI use more brick); traditional **grass roofs**
Plates long white + EU strip; **commercial plates green** (vans!) — most common in Europe here
hints.uniq_note: Green plates exist elsewhere only on EVs — frequent green VAN plates = Norway.

hints.text: Small roads: **long dashed white outer lines**; bigger roads: solid outer + **yellow middle w/ orange tinge**; lines often made of **paint dots**

shared_with.note: Finnish outer lines always solid; middles yellow or white.

hints.text: **Norwegian: Æ Ø Å** (Danish identical letters!); vs Swedish: more E's, 'kk' instead of 'ck'

shared_with.note: Danish uses Æ Ø Å too — near-identical on signs.
shared_with.diff: Flat Denmark vs mountains; Danish infra (rectangular line sections).

hints.text: Bollards: thin **curved rectangles, reflector inside a black parallelogram**
Snow poles orange w/ **thin reflector near the middle**; also thin **wooden snow poles with painted reflectors** (other Nordics: wooden ones lack reflectors)

shared_with.note: Finnish reflector at the very top.

hints.text: **Guardrails often on wooden posts** (not SE/FI); rusty guardrails → coasts (esp. southern west coast)
hints.uniq_note: Wooden guardrail posts are the quick Nordic separator — Sweden and Finland use steel.

hints.text: Pedestrian signs: 4 stripes + **very simply drawn person** (variants: 5 stripes, hatted person); SE = detailed person no hat, FI = 5 stripes

Directional signs **yellow w/ black border** + road number in green (E/major) or white (small) box; traffic signs = **white background + red outline** (SE/FI: yellow!); bright-yellow construction signs; small signs attached w/ **4 bolts in a tall rectangle**
Blue **M signs** (meeting place) — Swedish version's M font differs (middle point not to the bottom in NO); bus stops = small **blue sign w/ white bus**; 'Sone' speed-zone signs in cities
Chevrons **yellow on black**; white square km markers: road number (R = findable on the map, Fv = hard) + distance to next town
Street suffixes **-veien / -gata/-gate attached** (Sweden: väg/gata detached variants; Denmark: vej/gade); street signs **centred on top of posts** (Sweden: side-mounted)
Wooden poles w/ Nordic **black metal cap**; lamps mounted w/ **thick SINGLE bolting** (SE/FI: double)
Gen4 **smallcam** coverage
**Road-number first digit = region** (exceptions among 1–2-digit roads; 9 = road 9 N of Kristiansand + far north; Fv roads don't follow)
**Sámi bilingual signs** in the north: Á, Đ, several vowels in a row
**Vegetation**: bracken ferns → south; spruces → SE + Trondheim; pines → southern coasts + far inland; oaks → far south; maples → Oslo farmlands; bright moss → Lillehammer↔Oppdal
**Farmland triangle**: SE/Oslo = flattest, rolling w/ smooth forested hills; Trondheim = hilly & uneven; south of Stavanger (Jæren) = flat, vast open grass fields + **round-rock walls**

**Deep fjords + tall mountains** → Bergen↔Trondheim; **Lofoten** = sharp rocky low-tree mountains; NE = rolling, tiny birches + patchy grass; SW = barren treeless highlands; south coast = exposed rock w/ coastal pines; far-north coast = bare grasslands + low distant mountains; all-white villages → south coast
**Black snow poles** → Bergen↔Trondheim; small wide black pole plates → southeast; **Svalbard** = separate page here
**Oslo**: big black/red pole plates; **Stavanger**: two red stripes on lamp posts; **Bergen**: green fire hydrants
**Russian border**: humid green overcast/foggy road w/ low thin birches+pines; **Russian on signs** near Kirkenes (E6/E105); **Røros** = dense traditional wooden mining town
